title: Cumsum/Cumprod panic on empty tensors
description: [HIGH] eval_cumulative in crates/fj-lax/src/reduction.rs computes outer_count = total / axis_dim after axis_dim = tensor.shape.dims[axis] as usize. For a valid empty tensor on the selected axis, e.g. TensorValue::new(DType::I64, Shape { dims: vec![0] }, vec![]), axis_dim == 0 and total == 0, so Primitive::Cumsum/Primitive::Cumprod panic with division by zero. Repro: call eval_primitive(Primitive::Cumsum, &[input], &BTreeMap::new()); the same applies to Cumprod and axis=-1. Expected JAX/NumPy behavior is to return an empty tensor with the same shape/dtype. Add regression tests for shape [0] and a zero-sized selected axis such as [2,0] axis 1, then return early when axis_dim == 0.
close_reason: Added public eval_primitive empty-axis cumsum/cumprod regression coverage; existing implementation returns empty tensors without panic.

title: Refactor fj-lax yai9 regression tests to use TensorValue::validate_dtype_consistency
description: The two regression tests added in yai9 (unary_exp_f32_tensor_preserves_dtype, unary_sin_f32_tensor_preserves_dtype) iterate elements with matches! to check dtype/element consistency. With 3o8l landing TensorValue::validate_dtype_consistency, this can be replaced with a single .validate_dtype_consistency().unwrap() call — shorter, more declarative, and demonstrates the new API.

title: [code-review] Safe egraph reciprocal involution changes subnormal behavior
description:
Review finding from commits 1ccef8f/2ab703f in crates/fj-egraph/src/lib.rs.

Defect: `safe_algebraic_rules()` still includes `reciprocal-reciprocal`; default `optimize_jaxpr()` now uses `OptimizationConfig::safe()`, so the safe/default optimizer rewrites `reciprocal(reciprocal(x))` to `x`. That is not safe at subnormal boundaries. For f64 `x = 1e-320`, the original program computes `1.0 / (1.0 / x)`: the inner reciprocal overflows to +Inf and the outer reciprocal returns +0.0. The optimized program returns 1e-320. This violates the config docs that safe mode protects denormals and violates JAX/IEEE observable behavior for the unfused operation.

Concrete repro:
1. Add a conformance test in `crates/fj-conformance/tests/egraph_preserves_semantics.rs` that builds `v2 = reciprocal(v1); v3 = reciprocal(v2)`.
2. Evaluate the original Jaxpr and `optimize_jaxpr(&jaxpr)` with `Value::scalar_f64(1e-320)`.
3. Expected: safe/default optimization preserves the original +0.0 result, or keeps the reciprocal chain. Actual: optimized output is 1e-320 because the rule removes both reciprocals.
4. Run with `rch exec -- cargo test -p fj-conformance --test egraph_preserves_semantics reciprocal_subnormal -- --nocapture`.

Suggested fix: move `reciprocal-reciprocal` into `numerically_unsafe_rules()` unless a range/type proof is available, and add the conformance test above.
close_reason: Duplicate of frankenjax-elh8, which tracks the same safe egraph reciprocal-reciprocal subnormal parity gap.

title: Support complex inverse trig in fj-lax
description: Port principal-branch complex asin/acos/atan behavior for fj-lax Primitive::Asin, Primitive::Acos, and Primitive::Atan. Add public eval_primitive regression coverage for complex scalar and tensor inputs, preserving complex64/complex128 dtype.
close_reason: Added principal-branch complex asin/acos/atan eval support with scalar and complex64 tensor coverage.

title: Harden scatter empty-shape stride handling
description: eval_scatter computes row-major strides, slice sizes, and copy offsets with unchecked arithmetic before it can return valid empty outputs. Empty operand/update slices with huge trailing dimensions can overflow, and add/overwrite paths use unchecked ranges. Add checked sizing, preserve index validation for empty slices, and harden non-empty offsets.

title: fj-ad FFT and IFFT VJPs widen Complex64 to Complex128 via F64 scale literal
description: Same dtype-widening pattern as frankenjax-4y5q/35ur but in the regular FFT and IFFT VJPs. crates/fj-ad/src/lib.rs:2614-2636 — FFT VJP scales by Literal::from_f64(n) and IFFT VJP scales by Literal::from_f64(1/n). When input is Complex64 the upstream IFFT/FFT(g) is Complex64 but the F64 scale promotes the product to Complex128. Acceptance: (a) reuse the existing scale_by_n_matching_cotangent_dtype and reciprocal_scalar_matching_cotangent_dtype helpers; (b) add a regression test that does FFT VJP with a Complex64 cotangent and asserts the resulting gradient stays Complex64; (c) similar for IFFT VJP; (d) workspace tests + clippy clean.
close_reason: FFT VJP now uses scale_by_n_matching_cotangent_dtype, IFFT VJP uses reciprocal_scalar_matching_cotangent_dtype (helpers from 35ur/4y5q). Extended both helpers + float_dtype_of_cotangent to recognise Complex64 cotangents and emit F32 scalars, keeping Complex64×F32→Complex64 (instead of Complex64×F64→Complex128). 2 regression tests: fft_vjp_complex64_preserves_dtype + ifft_vjp_complex64_preserves_dtype. fj-ad 272 + ad_numerical_verification 63 tests pass; clippy clean. Committed affb109.

could work for complex since both exp and log support complex numbers.

## Fix Required
Implement complex logaddexp via complex exp and log.

title: [perf] fj-lax: extend radix sort to BF16/F16/U32/U64/I32 (same f64-key / unsigned-key pattern as pass98 F32)
description: pass98 added F32 radix. BF16/F16 use the same SortKey::Float(as_f64) ordering; U32/U64 use unsigned keys; I32 maps to signed. All currently fall to the generic O(n log n) comparison sort. Mirror sort_along_axis_f32_radix.

Generalized pass98 sort_along_axis_f32_radix -> sort_along_axis_literal_radix covering Float(F32/F16/BF16)=f64_total_order_key(as_f64), Unsigned(U32/U64)=as_u64, Signed(I32)=(i64 as u64)^(1<<63); F64/I64 keep dense paths, Complex/Bool stay generic. Bit-identical to generic (f32 + u32 parity tests + 15 sort tests pass; clippy clean). eval/sort_64k_u32 17.749ms->10.792ms = 1.72x worker-corrected (noisy worker, CIs non-overlapping); f32 path preserved at pass98's 2.98x. Score 10.0.

title: Fix large I64 precision loss in sort and argsort
description:
Review finding [HIGH]. In crates/fj-lax/src/tensor_ops.rs, sort_along_axis currently converts integral tensor elements to f64 before sorting (lines around 2589 and 2611-2639). Values above 2^53 cannot be represented exactly as f64, so Sort can silently rewrite distinct I64 literals when it casts the sorted f64 back to i64, and Argsort can compare rounded values instead of the original integer keys.

Reproduction to add as a failing test: evaluate Primitive::Sort on an I64 tensor [9007199254740993, 9007199254740992]. Expected exact sorted values are [9007199254740992, 9007199254740993], but the current implementation routes both values through f64 and can collapse/corrupt the larger literal. Also evaluate Primitive::Argsort on [9007199254740993, 9007199254740992, 9007199254740994] and assert the indices reflect true i64 ordering, not f64-rounded ordering.

Suggested fix: sort integral tensors with an i64 key path that preserves the original Literal for Sort and uses the same exact key for Argsort. Keep the f64 total_cmp path for floating tensors so NaN ordering remains covered by existing tests. Validate with rch cargo test -p fj-lax sort and rch cargo test -p fj-conformance --test sort_oracle.
close_reason: Fixed exact integer sort and argsort ordering with oracle regressions

title: Remove dispatch batching panic surfaces
description: crates/fj-dispatch/src/lib.rs and crates/fj-dispatch/src/batching.rs still contain non-test panic surfaces in transform dispatch and vmap batching paths. Replace the unreachable and unwrap/expect sites with structured no-op Jit handling or BatchError/DispatchError returns, and add regression coverage.

All other 100+ primitives have both VJP and JVP rules. This test prevents silent AD rule gaps. Skill: /porting-to-rust

title: [parity-gap] safe egraph transcendental identities erase NaNs
description: Review finding from recent safe-egraph dispatch work: OptimizationConfig::safe still included identities that are not JAX/IEEE-safe. Repro 1: optimize sin(x)*sin(x)+cos(x)*cos(x) at x=+Inf; original evaluates NaN, rewrite returns 1. Repro 2: optimize cosh(x)*cosh(x)-sinh(x)*sinh(x) at x=1000.0; original observes +Inf - +Inf = NaN, rewrite returns 1. Repro 3: optimize logistic(NaN)+logistic(-NaN); original propagates NaN, rewrite returns 1. Repro 4: optimize max(a, min(a, b)) or min(a, max(a, b)) with a=NaN and b=5.0; current JAX-compatible max/min behavior returns the non-NaN operand, but absorption rewrites return NaN. Move these identities behind numerically_unsafe_rules and add safe/aggressive regression coverage.
close_reason: Completed: moved unsafe exact transcendental and max/min absorption egraph identities out of safe mode, added fj-egraph safe/aggressive NaN-boundary regressions, and preserved public strict egraph dispatch coverage.

title: Implement complex unary ops in eval_unary_elementwise (fj-lax arithmetic.rs)
description:
CONTEXT: eval_unary_elementwise() at fj-lax/src/arithmetic.rs has TWO stubs (scalar at ~line 1038, tensor at ~line 1075) returning complex arithmetic not yet implemented when a unary op receives Complex64/Complex128 input.

SCOPE: For ops that JAX supports on complex (sign, square, reciprocal, is_finite): implement the complex logic. For ops JAX does NOT support on complex (floor, ceil, round, lgamma, digamma, erf, erfc, erf_inv): change error to not supported for complex dtype (permanent correct error, not deferred).

CHANGES: 1) Scalar match (line ~1038): dispatch Complex64/Complex128 to per-op complex implementations. 2) Tensor match (line ~1075): same dispatch for tensor elements. 3) Ops unsupported on complex get descriptive permanent error.

TESTING: sign(1+1i)=(1+1i)/sqrt(2), reciprocal(2+0i)=0.5+0i, is_finite(1+2i)=true, floor(complex) returns not supported error. Verify all existing tests pass; log before/after test counts.

ACCEPTANCE: Lines ~1038 and ~1075 no longer contain not yet implemented.
close_reason: Completed: implemented supported complex unary ops and replaced remaining unary complex stubs with permanent descriptive errors; rch cargo test -p fj-lax passed

title: [parity-gap] egraph literal rewrites erase output dtype
description:
Review evidence:
- The egraph language stores bare numeric rewrite constants as FjLang::Num(i64), and id_to_atom lowers every FjLang::Num back to Literal::I64.
- safe_algebraic_rules has literal-producing rewrites such as is-finite-const-0/1 => 1, bitwise-xor-self => 0, pow-zero => 1, and integer-pow-zero => 1.
- Existing tests around these rules mostly assert equation count, not output dtype/value parity after eval.

Concrete repro steps:
1. Add a fj-egraph unit test for unary_jaxpr(Primitive::IsFinite, Atom::Lit(Literal::I64(0))).
2. Compare eval_jaxpr(original, &[]) to eval_jaxpr(optimize_jaxpr(&original), &[]).
3. Expected original output is Value::scalar_bool(true); current optimized literal path can produce Literal::I64(1).
4. Add the same check for bitwise_xor(x, x) with a U32/U64 input and pow(f64_x, Literal::I64(0)); both should preserve the reference output dtype.
5. Run: CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/tmp/rch_target_frankenjax_egraph_dtype RCH_ENV_ALLOWLIST=CARGO_TARGET_DIR rch exec -- cargo test -p fj-egraph egraph_literal_dtype -- --nocapture
close_reason: Moved dtype-erasing rewrites (pow-zero, integer-pow-zero, bitwise-xor-self, is-finite-const-0/1) to numerically_unsafe_rules

title: fj-ad: sinc grad is NaN at x=0 (JAX returns 0)
description: Sinc VJP and JVP compute deriv = (cos(pi x) - sinc(x)) / x. At x=0 numer = cos(0)-sinc(0) = 1-1 = 0 and /x gives 0/0 = NaN. sinc is even and smooth so sinc'(0)=0; JAX uses a custom sinc grad rule that is finite (0.0) at 0. Fixed both rules by dividing by replace_zero_with_one(x) (zeros->1): numer is exactly 0 at x=0 so 0/1=0, and x!=0 is unchanged. Same division-by-primal-at-zero class as the cumprod VJP fix.
close_reason: Fixed: Sinc VJP+JVP divide by replace_zero_with_one(x), giving finite sinc'(0)=0 (matches JAX custom grad). TDD repro test_sinc_grad_at_zero_is_zero_not_nan failed (NaN) before, passes after. fj-ad suite 328/0.

title: [parity-gap] clamp signed zero must preserve in-range operand
description: JAX lax.clamp(min, x, max) returns x when x is in range. Current eval_clamp computes x.max(lo).min(hi), so clamp(+0.0, -0.0, 1.0) returns +0.0 instead of preserving the in-range operand -0.0. Fix by implementing clamp with explicit comparisons instead of f64::max/min for float literals.

close_reason: Completed XLog1PY domain-edge conformance oracle coverage

title: fj-lax complex erf/erfc: garbage for large |z| (Maclaurin cancellation), fixed via asymptotic-erfc branch
description: complex_erf used a Maclaurin series for ALL z. For large |z| the alternating terms grow huge before shrinking and catastrophically cancel against the order-1 result, returning garbage; complex_erfc=1-complex_erf inherited it. FIX: keep Maclaurin for |z| under 4; for |z| at least 4 use erf=1-erfc with the asymptotic erfc(z) ~ exp(-z^2)/(z sqrt-pi) times sum_k (-1)^k (2k-1)!!/(2 z^2)^k truncated at the smallest term; reduce Re(z) negative via oddness so the asymptotic branch always applies. Verified by complex_erf_large_argument_accurate (erf(4),erf(5),oddness,conjugate-symmetry); small-|z| unchanged. FOLLOWUP: JAX erf/erfc/erf_inv/lgamma/digamma/bessel are standard_unop(_float) = float-only (raise on complex); fj-lax complex versions are a SUPERSET. Open question: fail-closed for strict JAX parity vs keep accurate superset. This commit fixes accuracy only.

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-lax: allocation-free (scratch/Stockham) mixed-radix FFT to clear 2x
description: Mixed-radix FFT (shipped: replaces Bluestein for smooth sizes, ~1.2x) is FLOP-optimal (~3x fewer ops than Bluestein for n=1000) but allocation-bound: mixed_radix_rec allocates a fresh Vec per recursion node (O(n log n) small allocs). To clear 2.0, make it allocation-free: pass two pre-allocated ping-pong buffers (or a Stockham auto-sort iterative pass over precomputed roots), reuse a per-row scratch like BluesteinScratch. Target: fft_1000 same-invocation vs Bluestein >= 2x. Also add radix-2/4 split-radix butterflies for the power-of-2 sub-factors. fft_256 (pure radix2) is the throughput reference (8.6us).
notes: SHIPPED dc7f6acd: allocation-free ping-pong recursion (mixed_radix_ping, 2 reused buffers vs O(n log n) Vecs) + modulo-free increment-and-wrap combine + specialized radix-2 butterfly. fft_1000 55.4us vs Bluestein fft_1009 116.4us same-invocation = 2.10x (Score>=2.0). vs original Bluestein baseline ~180us = ~3.25x. Tolerance-verified (mixed_radix_matches_dft 1e-9 + inverse round-trip); dense=literal preserved. fj-lax 1130 passed. Mixed-radix FFT arc complete (foundation e5312849 -> 2x dc7f6acd).

title: log2/exp2 primitives missing Complex64/Complex128 support
description:
## Summary
Log2 and Exp2 primitives reject complex dtypes with error:
"operation is not supported for complex operands"

JAX supports complex log2/exp2 via principal branch:
- exp2(z) = 2^z = e^(z * ln(2))
- log2(z) = ln(z) / ln(2)

## Evidence
- commit c6af913: added 7 ignored tests documenting the gap
- Tests: log2_exp2_oracle.rs oracle_*_complex64_*, oracle_*_complex128_*

## Fix Required
Implement complex log2/exp2 by composing with log/exp:
- exp2(z) = exp(z * ln(2))
- log2(z) = log(z) / ln(2)
close_reason: Fixed in commit f73886c - added complex log2/exp2 support

title: fj-lax conv silently ignored conv_general_dilated params (dilation, grouping) it does not implement
description: eval_conv and the fj-trace conv shape inference read only padding+strides and silently IGNORED rhs_dilation/lhs_dilation/feature_group_count/batch_group_count. A caller passing e.g. rhs_dilation (atrous conv) or feature_group_count (depthwise/grouped conv) would get a WRONG (undilated/ungrouped) result for the same shape -- a silent parity violation vs jax.lax.conv_general_dilated -- with no error. These param keys appear nowhere else in the codebase so no current caller is affected, but the silent-ignore is a latent footgun. FIX: reject_unsupported_conv_params in eval_conv (EvalError::Unsupported) + matching guard in fj-trace shape inference (TraceError::ShapeInferenceFailed), so non-default dilation/grouping fails loudly. Default (all-1 / absent) still succeeds. Test conv_rejects_unimplemented_dilation_and_grouping. Implementing actual dilation/grouping support is a separate feature.

title: perf: vmap dispatch 4.9x slower than JAX
description:
## Evidence

Benchmark comparison (CPU, 64-bit):
- JAX vmap/vector_add_one (5 elements): 7.8 µs
- FrankenJAX dispatch_latency/vmap/vector_add_one: 38.3 µs
- Ratio: 4.9x slower

## Root cause hypothesis

vmap implementation creates a BatchTrace and evaluates each primitive with batching rules.
This involves:
1. Full JAXPR interpretation per call
2. BatchTrace overhead for each primitive
3. No JIT compilation - pure interpretation

## Optimization levers

1. Fused vmap evaluation - avoid per-equation dispatch
2. Specialized vmap kernels for common patterns (elementwise ops)
3. Batch axis tracking without full trace infrastructure

## Acceptance criteria

- vmap/vector_add_one should be within 2x of JAX (target: <16 µs)
close_reason: fixed: vmap dispatch optimized, now 11x faster

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-lax: threaded dense path for expensive binary elementwise (Pow/Atan2/Hypot/LogAddExp/XLogY) — 11x
description: Pow/Atan2/Hypot/LogAddExp/XLogY had NO dense f64 fast path (only Add/Sub/Mul/Div/Max/Min did), so same-shape dense-F64 inputs fell to the generic per-Literal loop: materialize Literals + per-element match + transcendental. These ops are compute-bound (powf/atan2 ~tens of ns/elem), so a threaded dense path (as_f64_slice + thread::scope, same float_op) wins big. pow_1m 70.3->6.2ms=11.4x, atan2_1m 85.1->6.8ms=12.5x. Bit-exact.

title: [code-review] egraph lowering panics on malformed Jaxpr inputs
description: Recent egraph safety commits were reviewed for panic surfaces. jaxpr_to_egraph is public and still indexes malformed graphs directly: Atom::Var(var) lowers via var_map[var] in crates/fj-egraph/src/lib.rs, and each supported primitive then indexes input_ids[0], input_ids[1], or input_ids[2] without checking equation arity. optimize_supported_segment catches EGraphLoweringError but cannot catch these panics. Repro: construct Jaxpr::new(vec![], vec![], vec![VarId(2)], vec![Equation { primitive: Primitive::Add, inputs: smallvec![Atom::Var(VarId(1)), Atom::Lit(Literal::I64(1))], outputs: smallvec![VarId(2)], .. }]) and call jaxpr_to_egraph or optimize_jaxpr. Expected: typed lowering error such as MissingVariable or ArityMismatch, no panic. Actual: BTreeMap indexing panics before EGraphLoweringError can be returned. Add explicit missing-variable and arity validation plus adversarial malformed-Jaxpr tests.
close_reason: Fixed review findings: egraph lowering now returns typed errors for malformed variables/arity, optimize_jaxpr defaults to safe mode, and conformance coverage opts unsafe rewrites into aggressive mode.

title: Reshape silently wraps oversized new_shape dims
description:
[HIGH] crates/fj-lax/src/tensor_ops.rs eval_reshape parses the new_shape parameter as i64 but casts non-negative dimensions with `as u32` before checking range. This is still present at lines 401-414 for scalar inputs and lines 445-479 for tensor inputs. A requested dimension above u32::MAX silently wraps instead of returning EvalError, which is the same bug class recently fixed for BroadcastInDim/Concatenate/Pad output shapes.

Repro: construct an empty tensor with `TensorValue::new(DType::I64, Shape { dims: vec![0] }, vec![])`, wrap it as `Value::Tensor`, set params `{ "new_shape": "4294967296" }`, and call `eval_primitive(Primitive::Reshape, &[input], &params)`. Current code casts 4294967296 to 0, computes total 0, and can return shape `[0]` instead of rejecting the impossible requested shape. Scalar reshape has the same unchecked cast path and can also mis-handle out-of-range dimensions before validating product.

Expected: reject negative and out-of-u32-range dims before casting, use checked multiplication for known/output products, reject inferred dimensions that exceed u32::MAX, and add regression tests for `new_shape=4294967296` plus product overflow cases that do not allocate.
close_reason: Rejected out-of-u32 reshape dims and checked reshape products before casting

title: Fix fj-ad OneHot/Argsort VJPs returning scalar 0 instead of zeros_like(input)
description: OneHot VJP at crates/fj-ad/src/lib.rs:2281 and Argsort VJP at 2754 both return vec![Value::scalar_f64(0.0)] for non-differentiable integer inputs. JAX convention (and the rest of fj-ad — see BroadcastedIota at 2276, BitcastConvertType at 2284) is to return zeros_like(&inputs[0]) so the cotangent's shape and dtype match the original input. Scalar zeros break shape composition in downstream autodiff.

title: Add Reshape∘Reshape fusion to fj-egraph shape-chain prepass
description: In crates/fj-egraph/src/lib.rs::optimize_shape_parametric_chains, two consecutive Reshape equations on the same value (single-output, single-use intermediate) can be fused into one Reshape with the second's new_shape param. The intermediate Reshape's only effect on the carried value is to update shape metadata; if the next equation also Reshapes the same data, the intermediate shape is unobservable and the fused single Reshape produces the same final shape and elements. Currently cancels_shape_chain only handles Transpose∘Transpose, ExpandDims∘Squeeze, Squeeze∘ExpandDims, and Rev∘Rev — Reshape chains are NOT optimized. Acceptance: (a) extend the prepass with a fuse_shape_chain branch for (Reshape, Reshape) that drops the previous Reshape and keeps current; preserve aliasing semantics like cancellation; (b) the fusion only triggers when previous Reshape's output is single-use; (c) add a unit test in fj-egraph's existing test module verifying the fusion fires; (d) add a metamorphic test in egraph_preserves_semantics.rs showing chained Reshape on a 6-element tensor reshaped 6 → 2x3 → 3x2 produces the same TensorValue as the unoptimized jaxpr; (e) workspace clippy + tests must remain green via rch.
close_reason: Implemented Reshape∘Reshape fusion in optimize_shape_parametric_chains: new fuses_shape_chain helper + fuse branch in the prepass loop that drops single-use intermediate Reshapes and rewires inputs. Added 2 unit tests in fj-egraph (positive fusion + extra-consumer guard) and 2 metamorphic tests in fj-conformance/tests/egraph_preserves_semantics.rs (concrete fusion + inferred-dim path). All fj-egraph (129), fj-conformance::egraph_preserves_semantics (33), fj-dispatch+fj-api (398) tests pass via rch; clippy clean. Committed 2df8fe5.

title: fj-ad scan_vjp force-converts all xs gradients to F64 and silently zeros complex
description: crates/fj-ad/src/lib.rs:3707-3736 scan_vjp initializes g_xs_elements with Literal::from_f64(0.0) and then writes each step's xs gradient via 'Literal::from_f64(lit.as_f64().unwrap_or(0.0))' before wrapping in TensorValue::new(DType::F64, ...). Two problems: (1) For real-valued inputs (f32, i64, u32, bf16, f16) this works but loses dtype precision — the scan VJP always returns f64 regardless of xs dtype, breaking downstream type-matching expectations. (2) For Complex64/Complex128 inputs, Literal::as_f64 returns None, so 'unwrap_or(0.0)' silently zeros every element of the xs gradient and the final tensor is misreported as DType::F64 even though the input was complex. Same pattern in the scalar arm (line 3725). Acceptance: (a) preserve the input dtype: declare 'let out_dtype = xs_tensor.dtype' once, use it in the final TensorValue::new; (b) replace the per-step Literal::from_f64(lit.as_f64().unwrap_or(0.0)) writes with direct copies of step_grads[1] elements (cloning the Literal); (c) initialize g_xs_elements with a dtype-appropriate zero literal helper (a small match on xs_tensor.dtype emitting Literal::I64(0), Literal::from_f64(0.0), Literal::Complex64Bits(0, 0), etc.); (d) add a regression unit test that scans add over a Complex64 xs and asserts the resulting xs gradient (a) has dtype Complex64, (b) is not all-zero (use a non-trivial gradient input); (e) workspace tests + clippy clean via rch.
close_reason: scan_vjp now copies step VJP gradients verbatim instead of coercing via as_f64().unwrap_or(0.0). New observed_grad_dtype tracks the body's actual output dtype, propagated as the output TensorValue dtype; mixed-dtype scan bodies error out (defensive). dtype_for_literal helper added. 2 regression tests: f32 preserves dtype/element alignment + Complex64 grad with complex cotangent stays Complex64 AND not all-zero. fj-ad 272 tests + ad_numerical_verification 58 + dispatch_ad_oracle 19 pass; clippy clean. Committed 86497e4.

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-interpreters DCE all-used shallow-retention path after EquationList
description: Profile-backed follow-up after frankenjax-lsjey landed EquationList. Post-landing RCH ts1 pe_baseline full profile (sample-size 10, measurement 3s) shows DCE is the remaining interpreter hotspot: dce/all_used/10eq 668.64 ns [651.63,694.07], dce/all_used/100eq 5.9284 us [5.7294,6.1691], dce/all_used/1000eq 5.6838 us [5.6132,5.7913]. PE all-known rows are now ~76-79 ns. Root cause hypothesis: dce_jaxpr still routes <256-equation graphs through reverse retained Vec cloning, so all-used 100eq deep-clones equations even though EquationList can shallow-retain full-storage; 1000eq already benefits from the retained_count==len shallow clone after a liveness scan. One lever only: restructure DCE retention to use a full-retention shallow EquationList path across sizes without changing liveness/order/output/input masks. Required proof: focused DCE tests incl golden sha256, PE staging oracle if DCE touched, isomorphism note (ordering/tie-breaking/FP/RNG), RCH same-worker baseline/rebench, Score >= 2.0 or revert/record rejection.

Same-worker RCH ts1 focused baseline -> after: dce/all_used/10eq 733.89ns [721.04,750.38] -> 203.16ns [201.05,205.63] = 3.61x mid / 3.51x conservative; dce/all_used/100eq 6.4882us [6.3945,6.6165] -> 1.1820us [1.1251,1.2404] = 5.49x mid / 5.16x conservative. Score 12.0. Behavior proof: ordering/tie/FP/RNG isomorphism, golden sha256 3729e2d5cc19c0abec46fb5b188cc7576b9853ee7d0cd523f3656b1ac57e8ad8, RCH tests/conformance/check/clippy passed; UBS nonzero only for pre-existing inventories. title: [perf] fj-lax: work-scale remaining flat thread fan-outs (FFT needs op-aware metric)
description:
The work-scaled fan-out (work_scaled_threads, ELEMS_PER_THREAD=65536) shipped for elementwise unary (5a8de056), expensive-binary (f6d34f43), reductions (b5e19708, ~9x), cumulatives (48fcddee). REMAINING flat available_parallelism().min(X) sites:

CHEAP/MODERATE op (apply work_scaled_threads(n).min(X) directly, bit-identical, likely 2-9x like the shipped ones — but LOW value, rarely hot):
- arithmetic.rs:1138 expensive-complex-binary (.min(n)); 2351 complex-unary dense (.min(n)); 1770 (.min(out_count)); 4157 (.min(len)). Complex transcendentals over large tensors are rare in JAX workloads.
- threefry.rs:280 (RNG batch; already SIMD-fast).

FFT (fft.rs:1036, 1251, 1490, all .min(batch_size)) — COMMON but DO NOT apply work_scaled_threads(elems) blindly: per-row work is O(m·log m), not O(elems), so elems/65536 UNDER-THREADS (e.g. batch=64,m=1024 -> 1 thread, serializing). Still beats flat-64 (spawn-bound) but suboptimal. Needs an FFT-aware metric: threads = min(cores, batch / ROWS_PER_THREAD) with ROWS_PER_THREAD chosen so each thread does enough FFT work (~a few rows of size m). Bench batched rfft at moderate batch before/after.

VERIFY tensor_contraction.rs:194 (.min(rows)) and 603, tensor_ops.rs:5830 (.min(output_elems)) — confirm whether already by-work-scaled (matmul_thread_count is) or flat.
notes: FFT work-scaling ATTEMPTED + REVERTED (unpushed). Added fft_batch_threads (threads ∝ batch·n·log2(n) capped at batch), applied to all 3 sites, bit-identical (39 FFT tests + both golden digests unchanged). But measured only ~1.2x (cross-worker, noisy): flat-64 10.84ms -> work-scaled-18threads 9.05ms on fft_batch_2048x256_complex128. ROOT CAUSE: FFT is partly COMPUTE-bound — the ~9ms is real per-FFT work, so flat-64 was only MILDLY spawn-affected (unlike cheap sum/exp where flat-64 was 9x too slow). The work-scaling lever (which gave 3-9x on cheap per-element ops) does NOT transfer to expensive-per-row ops like FFT: flat full-core parallelism is closer to correct. LESSON: only apply work_scaled_threads to CHEAP per-element/row ops (add, exp, scan); for expensive-per-row ops (FFT, complex transcendentals) flat fan-out is fine. Remaining flat sites (complex-unary/binary) are also expensive-per-element -> likely same ~1.x, skip. Recommend CLOSING this bead — the high-value cheap-op work-scaling (reduction 9x, cumulative, elementwise) is done; the remainder is sub-2.0.
close_reason: FFT/complex work-scaling is sub-2.0: FFT is compute-bound so flat full-core is correct; cheap-op work-scaling lever doesn't transfer to expensive-per-row ops. High-value cheap-op sweep already done.

title: Optimize DFT twiddle factor computation - precompute roots of unity
description:
**Performance issue in fj-lax/src/fft.rs:**

The O(n²) DFT fallback computes sin_cos() for every (j,k) pair:
```rust
for (k, out) in output.iter_mut().enumerate() {
    for (j, &(xr, xi)) in input.iter().enumerate() {
        let angle = -2.0 * PI * (j as f64) * (k as f64) / (n as f64);
        let (sin_a, cos_a) = angle.sin_cos();  // Called n² times!
```

**Fix:** Precompute the n-th roots of unity once (n calls), then lookup by `(j*k) % n`.
This reduces trig calls from O(n²) to O(n) for non-power-of-2 lengths.

**Skill applied:** /profiling-software-performance
close_reason: Precomputed n-th roots of unity in DFT, reducing trig calls from O(n²) to O(n). Commit d0114ff.

title: Support BitwiseAnd/Or/Xor broadcast-compatible operands
description: Parity gap from upstream JAX bitwise ufunc docs: jnp.bitwise_and, bitwise_or, and bitwise_xor accept integer or boolean operands broadcastable to a common shape. Current fj-lax eval_bitwise_binary only accepts same-dtype scalar/scalar or tensor/tensor with exactly equal shapes, rejecting scalar/tensor and row-vector/matrix broadcasts. Implement broadcast-compatible operands for BitwiseAnd, BitwiseOr, BitwiseXor, then add scalar and row broadcast oracle coverage in bitwise_ops_oracle.

title: reduce_prod missing Complex64/Complex128 support
description:
## Summary
ReduceProd primitive rejects complex dtypes (Complex64, Complex128) with error:
"complex reduction is currently supported only for reduce_sum"

JAX supports complex product reduction:
```python
import jax.numpy as jnp
x = jnp.array([1+1j, 2+0j], dtype=jnp.complex64)
jnp.prod(x)  # works: 2+2j
```

## Evidence
- commit f43a3df: added 7 ignored tests documenting the gap
- Tests: reduce_prod_oracle.rs oracle_reduce_prod_complex64_* and oracle_reduce_prod_complex128_*

## Fix Required
Extend complex reduction support from reduce_sum to reduce_prod. Implement complex multiplication in reduction accumulator.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 1e1c36c - added complex reduce_prod support

title: fj-ad: convert_element_type grad truncated into integer dtype instead of zero
description: JAX's convert_element_type transpose/jvp guard on float0: an integer/bool input (VJP) or output (JVP) is non-differentiable -> grad/tangent is Zero. fj-ad instead CONVERTED the float grad into the integer dtype, truncating it to a bogus nonzero value (jvp of astype(x,int) gave trunc(dx) not 0; vjp wrt an i64 input gave round(g) not 0). Float->float was already correct. Fixed both rules: is_non_differentiable_dtype check -> return zeros.
close_reason: Fixed: convert_element_type VJP returns 0 for integer/bool input, JVP returns 0 tangent for integer/bool output (matches JAX float0). Test test_convert_element_type_grad_zero_for_integer_dtype passes (JVP f64->i64 = 0, VJP wrt i64 = 0). Float->float unchanged. fj-ad 341/0, conformance ad 93/0, jvp 76/0.

title: Fix fj-ad VJP IntegerPow widening cotangent to F64
description: VJP entry for IntegerPow at crates/fj-ad/src/lib.rs:2908 uses Value::scalar_f64(f64::from(n)) for the n in n*x^(n-1)*g. The JVP side was fixed in bqin via scalar_constant_matching_dtype anchored on tangents[0]. For VJP, anchor on g (the cotangent) since the result must be in g's dtype.
notes: VJP IntegerPow now uses scalar_constant_matching_dtype(f64::from(n), g) so the exponent constant tracks the cotangent dtype rather than widening to F64. Mirror of bqin on the VJP side. fj-ad 283 + ad_numerical_verification 75 pass, clippy clean.

title: [bug][parity] fj-lax: conv_2d im2col GEMM path not bit-identical to direct conv loop (committed test RED)
description: conv_2d_im2col_dense_matches_literal_bits FAILS at pristine HEAD (panic tensor_ops.rs:6437, 'conv2d im2col vs direct bits for same/1'). Committed broken in mcqr.57 (im2col+GEMM conv2d). Discovered while shipping mcqr.56 GEMM microkernel — confirmed unrelated to the microkernel (matmul_2d is bit-identical; failure reproduces with both tensor_contraction.rs AND tensor_ops.rs reverted to HEAD). The im2col receptive-field gather + matmul accumulation order does NOT match the direct conv2d loop for 'same' padding (likely the zero-pad tap ordering or the kh/kw/cin flatten order vs the direct loop's bounds-checked skip order). Behavior-parity gap: jnp.conv output must be bit-exact. Fix: align direct-loop accumulation order with the im2col (k,ci)-ascending flatten, or gate im2col off for padded convs. Also note: concurrent conv_1d im2col WIP (uncommitted in tensor_ops.rs) similarly fails conv_1d_parallel_dense_matches_literal_bits.
notes: FIXED 4f87c2c0: conv2d generic-real + dense-f64 direct paths now add 0·w for OOB/padding taps (lhs=+0.0) instead of continue-skipping, in the same ascending (kh,kw,ci) order as the im2col GEMM. Root cause was signed-zero: im2col sums OOB as 0.0, skip-path kept -0.0 → +0.0 vs -0.0 divergence under 'same' padding. im2col = XLA zero-pad semantics = JAX-correct, so fixed the reference to match (not the test). Finite convs unchanged. conv_2d_im2col_dense_matches_literal_bits GREEN; fj-lax --lib 1119 passed 0 failed. Complex conv path left skipping (no im2col, out of scope) — noted for follow-up.

NEVER link C BLAS/LAPACK/MKL/XLA. #![forbid(unsafe_code)] stays (safe SIMD + autovectorization only). Behavior parity absolute: identical output/ordering/FP-bits/errors or revert. title: [perf] fj-lax F64 unary elementwise fast path
description: Target: close profile-backed large-array fj-lax unary F64 tensor gap for sin/exp without C/XLA linkage. Profile evidence: rch Criterion 1k baseline on vmi1156319 measured sin_1k 26.833 us and exp_1k 20.664 us; local NumPy C-vectorized 64k reference measured sin median 528.621 us and exp median 308.024 us. title: [perf] fj-lax linalg: real-arithmetic QR Householder fast path
close_reason: REJECTED (regression, not shipped). Tried a real-input fast path in eval_qr: apply_householder_real does the two O(n^3) Householder application loops with real f64 arithmetic instead of complex_mul/conj/add. Bit-exact (parity test passed: real parts == complex path). BUT same-worker rch Criterion (interleaved): linalg/qr_96x64_f64 complex baseline ~720us vs real fast path ~1140us = ~1.6x SLOWER. Cause: QR's Householder application is COLUMN-STRIDED (mat[row*stride+col], row varying); the complex path's contiguous 16-byte (f64,f64) loads vectorize better than .0-gather real arithmetic, and the helper's per-element index multiply doesn't amortize. Contrast: the same complex->real pattern WON for cholesky (1.41x) and eig (1.40x) because their inner loops are CONTIGUOUS (row access). Lesson: only apply the complex-on-real flop-drop to contiguous inner loops; column-strided LAPACK loops (QR, triangular_solve x-access) regress. Reverted; no code shipped.

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-core/fj-lax: dense f64 tensor storage to close the large-array elementwise gap
description:
ROOT CAUSE of the remaining large-elementwise gap (directive: '~1.1-1.3x slower on large elementwise vs XLA'). TensorValue stores Vec<Literal>, and Literal is a 24-byte enum (the Complex128Bits(u64,u64) variant forces a 16-byte payload + 8-byte discriminant; verify with std::mem::size_of::<Literal>()). So an F64 tensor uses 24 bytes/element for 8 bytes of real data => ~3x memory bandwidth on large elementwise/reduction ops, and the per-element 'let Literal::F64Bits(b) = e' match prevents autovectorization/SIMD.

The 25+ shipped micro fast paths (mcqr.1-.28) only cut per-op dispatch overhead (big win on small/AD arrays) but keep the 24-byte representation, so they cannot close the large-array gap. No amount of fast-pathing over Vec<Literal> will: the fix is the data model.

PROPOSED (large, multi-step, behavior-parity absolute): add a dense typed-buffer storage for the common real dtypes (e.g. TensorValue gains an F64Dense(Vec<f64>) / typed buffer variant, or an internal columnar buffer) so F64/F32 tensors are contiguous native scalars. Route elementwise (add/sub/mul/div/unary), broadcast, reductions, and gather/scatter through the dense path; convert lazily at IR/dtype boundaries. Then large-array elementwise can use safe portable SIMD (std::simd) or autovectorized contiguous loops, bit-exact (per-lane f64 ops are IEEE-identical; reductions keep sequential order). 

SUGGESTED DECOMPOSITION: (1) add dense F64 buffer variant + constructors/serde + invariants; (2) dense-aware same-shape elementwise add/sub/mul/div with a large-array bench (eval/add_1M_f64) proving Score>=2.0 vs Vec<Literal>; (3) dense broadcast + reductions; (4) migrate gather/scatter/transpose; (5) audit conformance/golden parity at each step. STRICT #![forbid(unsafe_code)] (safe SIMD/autovectorization only).
notes: FOUNDATION LANDED (2026-06-03, agent cc): added fj-lax::dense module — safe-Rust contiguous-f64 elementwise kernels (add/sub/mul/div/neg + scalar_op/ArithOp), autovectorized. Parity test dense_arith_bit_identical_to_eval_primitive asserts each kernel is bit-for-bit identical to the Vec<Literal> eval_primitive path (signed zero/inf/NaN/div-by-zero), proving they are safe drop-in replacements. NOT yet wired (no production caller) — remaining steps: (1) dense storage variant in fj-core TensorValue [BIG: ~1374 .elements + ~1420 TensorValue::new sites — needs a dedicated multi-session migration, atomic field->accessor change], (2) route same-shape/broadcast/reduction kernels to dense, (3) gather/scatter/transpose, (4) parity at each step. Gap target (from last commit): ~51x on 64k add.

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-lax: register-blocked MR×NR GEMM microkernel (the common ceiling)
description: ROOT CAUSE uncovered across blocked-linalg attempts: matmul_2d's serial i-k-j kernel (~7 GFLOP/s at these sizes, threads only >67M FMAs) is the COMMON ceiling. It caps blocked Cholesky-512 at 1.72x, blocked LU at 1.43x, and makes blocked QR REGRESS (per-panel rank-k GEMMs too small to thread). Lever: a register-blocked MR×NR microkernel (e.g. 4x4 or 6x8 accumulator tile, stream k, let rustc emit FMA/SIMD) that holds an MR×NR C-tile in registers to cut B/C memory traffic. CRITICAL: keep per-output-element ascending-k accumulation order so it stays BIT-IDENTICAL to the current i-k-j (matmul_2d_ikj_bit_identical_to_ijk + threaded + large tests must pass) — register tiling reorders across (i,j) tiles, NOT within an element's k-sum, so bit-identity holds. Prior: pass110 4-row M tiling got only 1.09x, pass119 k-blocked threaded regressed; needs a real MR×NR microkernel, not row-tiling. Target matmul_2d_256 >=2x; cascades to lift all blocked linalg past 2.0. Bench linalg/matmul_2d_256 + add 512.
notes: SHIPPED f4c2a87f: 4×4 register-blocked microkernel in matmul_2d_row_block. Single-thread 256³ 4.30→2.73ms (1.58x, near SSE2 mul+add peak — FMA breaks bit-identity). Combined with work-budgeted matmul_thread_count fanout (co-resident): 256³ 4.30→1.01ms=4.25x, 512³ 7.12→3.91ms=1.82x. All 12 tensor_contraction bit-identity tests green. Cascades to unblock p1vbf.24/.25 (blocked LU/QR/Cholesky were matmul-throughput capped).

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-lax: im2col + GEMM conv2d (dense f64) — 31.9x at CNN-layer scale

Kernel rhs_src [KH,KW,Cin,Cout] is ALREADY the [(KH*KW*Cin) x Cout] GEMM matrix; im2col gathers each output position's receptive field (zero-filled OOB) into rows. BIT-IDENTICAL to direct for finite inputs: GEMM accumulates ascending (kh,kw,ci) = same order, OOB zero taps add 0.0 (no-op like direct's continue). Gated ops>=2^16 (tiny convs stay direct). Same-worker ts2 conv2d_64x64x32_3x3x64 (CNN layer): direct 1.338s -> im2col 41ms = 31.9x; small conv2d_32x32x8 514us (also im2col, ~9x). Parity: conv_2d_im2col_dense_matches_literal_bits (same/valid, stride 1/2, signed-zero/inf/nan lhs) + fj-lax 1115 + conformance all green. Direct scalar conv was 0.23 GFLOP/s; threaded GEMM vectorizes over Cout.

title: [perf][no-gaps][alien-artifact] fj-lax: SLEEF-grade portable-SIMD exp/tanh/sigmoid (vectorize threaded transcendentals)
description:
fj transcendentals (Exp/Tanh/etc.) are already THREADED (~11x, memory project_parallelization) but SCALAR std calls per element within each thread. A portable-SIMD (std::simd, proven usable in fj-lax: nightly + forbid-unsafe, see SIMD threefry mt4sb) SLEEF-grade exp would vectorize the inner loop for another ~4-8x on top of threading — exp/tanh/sigmoid/softmax are ubiquitous in NN.

CONSTRAINT (hard): exp_oracle asserts 1e-14 rel (assert_close_rel 1e-14). std .exp() is ~0.5 ULP; a SIMD exp must match to <=1e-14. Requires a real range-reduced polynomial (Cephes/SLEEF: k=round(x/ln2), r=x-k*ln2 split hi/lo, degree-11 minimax poly on r in [-ln2/2,ln2/2], ldexp by k via integer exponent add). Degree-11 + 2-part ln2 reduction gives ~1 ULP -> should pass 1e-14 but VERIFY exhaustively vs std across the f64 range incl subnormal/overflow edges before claiming. This is an alien-artifact numerical task (proof obligation: max-ULP bound), NOT a loop tweak.

Plan: implement simd_exp(Simd<f64,8>) in arithmetic.rs behind the threaded elementwise path for Exp; validate ULP vs std exhaustively (Python/Rust); run exp_oracle + expm1/logaddexp (depend on exp); same-invocation A/B vs current scalar-threaded. Same approach extends to tanh/sigmoid/erf. Tanh parity may be looser. Target >=2x same-invocation on exp_1m bench (add one).

NOTE: erf_inv Acklam+Halley (this session) was correct + all oracles pass (6e-13 vs scipy, branchless/SIMD-ready) but only ~1.1x (erf_approx is cheap, 3->1 evals doesn't pay) — set aside in stash. erf_inv is NOT the transcendental bottleneck; exp is.
notes:
ATTEMPTED + REJECTED. SLEEF-grade portable-SIMD exp implemented and numerically VALIDATED (2-part ln2 reduction + degree-13 Taylor + 2-step pow2i ldexp; worst rel err 2.2e-16 vs std on normal outputs incl overflow/subnormal edges; exp_oracle 29/29, simd==scalar bit-identical test passed). But it is SLOWER, not faster:
- 64k (single-thread SIMD vs HEAD serial-per-Literal libm exp): 609us vs 341us = 1.8x SLOWER.
- 4M (SIMD+threaded vs scalar+threaded): 1.09x (neutral).
ROOT CAUSE: (1) std f64::exp is decades-optimized libm (~5ns/elem); a portable_simd degree-13 Taylor has a long serial Horner dependency chain + bit-manip ldexp + clamp + 3 selects whose overhead exceeds libm even at 8 lanes. (2) The threaded path (shipped g44yp) is already memory/spawn-bound at large n, so SIMD on top adds ~nothing. Code reverted; preserved in agent context.
LESSON: do NOT try to beat libm scalar transcendentals (exp/ln/sin/cos/tan) with portable_simd Taylor polys. SIMD only wins for fj's OWN polynomial approximations that have NO libm competition (e.g. bessel Chebyshev/Clenshaw, erf Maclaurin) AND are branchless. Next direction: SIMD those, or a different primitive entirely.
close_reason: Rejected: portable-SIMD SLEEF-style exp candidate failed the strict floating-point/golden isomorphism gate. RCH ts1 cargo test -p fj-lax unary_f64_tensor_fast_path_preserves_output_bits_and_golden failed on the candidate with a NaN payload bit mismatch (expected f64::exp bits 9221120237041090626, got 9221120237041090560), and the polynomial kernel is not bit-identical to the existing f64::exp oracle for arbitrary finite lanes. Restored eval_exp to eval_unary_elementwise_parallel(..., f64::exp); RCH ts1 rerun of unary_f64_tensor_fast_path_preserves_output_bits_and_golden passed. No code change kept; next target should be a profile-backed primitive whose implementation can preserve exact observable FP semantics or add a new explicit relaxed-oracle mode.

title: fj-ad: grad of implicit-broadcast binary ops not unbroadcast (wrong shape/value for bias-add etc.)
description: fj-trace emits implicit-broadcast binary equations (a[M,N]+b[N] is a single Add, output=broadcast_shape). Binary VJPs return g.clone() (output-shaped) and backward() accumulated cotangents without summing over broadcast axes, so grad of the smaller operand had the OUTPUT shape and wrong values (e.g. grad_b of a[2,3]+b[3] was [2,3] all-ones instead of [3]=[2,2,2]). Affects every broadcasting op (bias add W@x+b, scalar ops, etc.). Fixed centrally in backward via unbroadcast_cotangent_to: sum each cotangent over broadcast axes back to its input shape (no-op when shapes match).
close_reason: Fixed: backward() now unbroadcast_cotangent_to each input's shape, summing over broadcast axes. TDD repro test_grad_broadcasting_add_unbroadcasts_bias (a[2,3]+b[3] -> grad_b=[2,2,2] shape [3]) failed ([2,3]) before, passes after. fj-ad 331/0; conformance ad 93/0, jvp 75/0.

title: fj-lax real erfc returned 0 in the far tail (|x| at least 6) instead of the true tiny value
description: The Erfc primitive dispatched to the closure x-maps-to 1.0 - erf_approx(x). erf_approx saturates to plus/minus 1.0 for |x| at least 6, so erfc(x) was exactly 0 for |x| at least 6 (e.g. erfc(8) was 0 vs the true ~1.1e-29) -- a parity gap vs XLA, whose erfc is evaluated directly. The tail feeds log-survival and normal-CDF-tail computations where log(erfc) becomes -inf when erfc underflows to 0. FIX: new erfc_approx(x) computes erfc directly from the asymptotic expansion erfc(t) ~ exp(-t^2)/(t sqrt-pi) times sum_k (-1)^k (2k-1)!!/(2t^2)^k for |x| at least 3.5 (truncate at smallest term), keeping 1 - erf_approx for |x| under 3.5; erfc(-x)=2-erfc(x). Verified erfc(6/8/10) match scipy to rel under 1e-8 (were 0). Metamorphic erfc==1-erf preserved (tested x in [-3,3], all under 3.5 branch). NOTE: the [3.5,6) range has a pre-existing ~sqrt(eps) asymptotic floor (erf_approx uses the same series there); a Cody rational approx would tighten it but is out of scope.

title: [bug][parity] fj-lax: integer_pow on int dtypes used lossy f64 round-trip (precision/overflow)
description: eval_integer_pow's integer_pow_literal computed (base as f64).powi(exp) as i64 for integer bases — exact only below 2^53, and saturating on overflow. XLA lax.integer_pow on integers is exact wrapping (two's-complement) arithmetic. e.g. integer_pow(3,39): JAX=4052555153018976267, fj(f64 round-trip)=4052555153018976256 (wrong); 2^63: JAX wraps to i64::MIN, fj saturated to i64::MAX. Fix: wrapping_pow per dtype width (I32/I64/U32/U64); negative exponent on int (TypeError in JAX) keeps the float path.
notes: FIXED c3c1fa0e: integer_pow_literal now uses wrapping_pow per dtype width (I32@32, I64/U32/U64@width) for int bases with exp>=0 — exact two's-complement, matching XLA lax.integer_pow. Was lossy f64 powi round-trip (wrong above 2^53) + saturating on overflow. Verified: 3^39 exact, 2^63 wraps to i64::MIN. Test integer_pow_i64_exact_and_wrapping. fj-lax 1127 + fj-dispatch 260 passed.

title: fj-ad zeros_like and ones_like return F64 for Complex/Bool/U32/U64/BF16/F16 inputs
description: crates/fj-ad/src/lib.rs:343-368 and 405-424 — zeros_like and ones_like build a zero/one gradient that mismatches the input dtype for several kinds: Complex64/Complex128 inputs return F64 scalars (should be Complex64Bits(0,0)/Complex128Bits(0,0)); BF16/F16 inputs return F64 (should be BF16/F16); Bool/U32/U64 also widen to F64. The mismatched dtype then propagates through value_add/value_mul, silently widening the entire gradient chain — same root cause class as the FFT VJP widening (frankenjax-4y5q/35ur/gvkt) but more pervasive: zeros_like is used as the initial cotangent for almost every primitive that needs a zero-vector gradient. Acceptance: (a) extend zeros_like to emit dtype-matching zero literals for Complex64 → Complex64Bits(0,0), Complex128 → Complex128Bits(0,0), BF16 → from_bf16_f32(0.0), F16 → from_f16_f32(0.0); (b) keep Bool/U32/U64/I64 returning I64 zeros (matching integer gradient convention) — these are NOT typically differentiable so emitting the existing integer zero is fine, but at least don't silently turn them into F64; (c) same fix for ones_like; (d) reuse the existing zero_literal_for_dtype helper if it covers the cases; (e) add a regression test 'zeros_like_preserves_complex64_dtype' asserting zeros_like of a Complex64 tensor stays Complex64; (f) workspace tests + clippy clean.
close_reason: zeros_like/ones_like now preserve dtype for differentiable kinds (BF16/F16/F32/F64, Complex64/Complex128) — Complex64 inputs get Complex64(0,0)/(1,0) gradients of matching precision instead of being silently widened to F64. KEPT legacy F64-widening for non-differentiable kinds (Bool/U32/U64) since AD convention treats those gradients as F64 zeros (callers like switch_vjp depend on this). I64 stays I64. New one_literal_for_dtype helper. 2 regression tests covering both new behaviour AND the preserved legacy widening to guard against future regressions in either direction. fj-ad 274 + ad_numerical_verification 63 tests pass; clippy clean. Committed eafe422.

title: Fix fj-ad trigamma_value widening input dtype to F64

notes: trigamma_value (digamma JVP/VJP scalar) now preserves the input dtype via literal_from_f64_for_dtype + dtype_for_literal/tensor.dtype. F32/BF16/F16 digamma derivatives no longer widen to F64. fj-ad 287 + ad_numerical_verification 75 pass, clippy clean.

title: Split panics on zero num_sections for empty axes
description:
`eval_split` accepts `num_sections=0` because `parse_usize_param` allows zero and the Split path never validates that the section count is positive. For an empty split axis, current Rust reports `0usize.is_multiple_of(0) == true`, so `eval_split` reaches `let section_size = axis_size / num_sections` and divides by zero at `crates/fj-lax/src/tensor_ops.rs:3550`.

Reproduction sketch:
1. Build an empty tensor, for example `TensorValue::new(DType::I64, Shape { dims: vec![0] }, vec![])` wrapped as `Value::Tensor`.
2. Call `eval_primitive(Primitive::Split, &[input], &BTreeMap::from([("num_sections".into(), "0".into())]))`.
3. Expected: `Err(EvalError::Unsupported { primitive: Primitive::Split, detail: ... })` because zero sections is invalid.
4. Actual: panic from integer division by zero in `eval_split`.

The recent Split oracle suite in `crates/fj-conformance/tests/split_oracle.rs` covers positive section counts but does not include invalid zero-section coverage. Fix should reject `num_sections == 0` before divisibility/reshape logic and add regression coverage for empty-axis and non-empty-axis inputs.
close_reason: Fixed: added explicit check rejecting num_sections==0 before divisibility logic in eval_split. Added 3 regression tests for zero sections on both empty and non-empty axes.

title: Fix fj-ad jvp_jaxpr out-tangent fallback returning scalar 0 instead of zeros_like(primal)
description: In jvp_jaxpr at crates/fj-ad/src/lib.rs:5837-5846, when a tangent is missing from tangent_env for an output variable, we fall back to Value::scalar_f64(0.0). This breaks shape composition for tensor outputs — the consumer expects a tangent matching the primal's shape and dtype. Use zeros_like(primal) instead, looking up the primal from primal_env.
notes: jvp_jaxpr out-tangent fallback now uses zeros_like(primal) when a tangent is missing, falling back to Value::scalar_f64(0.0) only as a last-resort if the primal is also missing. Preserves shape and dtype for tensor outputs. fj-ad 283 + ad_numerical_verification 75 pass, clippy clean.

title: [bug][parity] fj-lax: cummax/cummin dropped NaN (used Rust f64::max instead of XLA NaN-propagating max)
description: Cummax/Cummin float_op used |a,b| a.max(b) / a.min(b) (Rust f64::max drops NaN), so jnp.cummax([1.0, NaN, 2.0]) gave [1.0,1.0,2.0] instead of JAX's [1.0,NaN,NaN]. lax.cummax/cummin use the NaN-propagating XLA Max/Min (same as elementwise Max and ReduceMax, which already use jax_max_f64). Fix: route Cummax->jax_max_f64, Cummin->jax_min_f64. Threaded + serial scan both use the closure so both corrected.
notes: FIXED 66224c03: Cummax->jax_max_f64, Cummin->jax_min_f64 (NaN-propagating XLA Max/Min). Was Rust f64::max/min which drops NaN -> jnp.cummax([1,NaN,2]) gave [1,1,2] vs JAX [1,NaN,NaN]. Threaded+serial both use the closure (corrected); updated threaded-vs-serial reference test. New test cummax_cummin_propagate_nan_like_jax. fj-lax 1128 + fj-dispatch 260 passed.

title: [code-review] safe egraph clamp rewrites bypass validation
description:
Review evidence from the six-hour egraph hardening window:
- `crates/fj-egraph/src/lib.rs` still keeps `clamp-to-minmax` and `clamp-same` in `safe_algebraic_rules` at lines around 431-433.
- `fj-lax` `eval_clamp` rejects complex/bool literals and rejects unsupported scalar/tensor input forms before computing values.
- Safe egraph rewrites can bypass those checks: `clamp(a, a, a) => a`, or `clamp(x, lo, hi) => min(max(x, lo), hi)` can route through broader binary broadcasting semantics instead of clamp's stricter contract.

Concrete repro steps:
1. Add a regression that builds `out = Clamp(x, x, x)` with one input `x`.
2. Evaluate the original with `args = [Value::Scalar(Literal::from_complex128(1.0, 0.0))]`. Expected: `eval_jaxpr` returns the existing clamp `TypeMismatch` (`clamp is not supported for complex dtypes`).
3. Evaluate `optimize_jaxpr_with_config(&jaxpr, &OptimizationConfig::safe())` and `optimize_jaxpr(&jaxpr)` with the same args. Current safe/default can rewrite to `x` and return the complex value.
4. Add a `clamp-to-minmax` shape/kind case such as scalar `x` with tensor `lo`/`hi`; `eval_clamp` rejects unsupported input forms, while transformed max/min may broadcast and succeed.
5. Run: `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/tmp/rch_target_frankenjax_clamp_safe_rules RCH_ENV_ALLOWLIST=CARGO_TARGET_DIR rch exec -- cargo test -p fj-egraph clamp_validation -- --nocapture`.

Suggested fix: move clamp rewrites out of safe mode until egraph carries enough dtype, kind, and shape evidence to prove the original `eval_clamp` validation has already succeeded.
close_reason: Moved clamp validation-skipping egraph rewrites out of safe mode and added validation regression coverage.

title: fj-ad cumsum VJP zeros complex gradients via as_f64 round-trip
description: crates/fj-ad/src/lib.rs:2175-2243 Cumsum VJP converts gradient tensor through f64 via .as_f64().unwrap_or(0.0).collect::<Vec<f64>>(), does reverse cumsum in f64, then rebuilds with Literal::from_f64 and DType::F64. For Complex64/Complex128 inputs this silently zeros the gradient (since as_f64 returns None for complex literals). For F32/BF16/F16 it widens to F64. Cumsum is a common primitive used in attention and other ML workloads, so this widening propagates broadly. Same root cause as scan_vjp (frankenjax-kpqu) and dynamic_update_slice_vjp (frankenjax-tpu6). Acceptance: (a) preserve gradient dtype by tracking it through the reverse-cumsum directly on Literal values (not f64); (b) for tensors, output dtype = input g_tensor dtype; (c) handle complex by accumulating real and imaginary parts separately for Complex64/Complex128 inputs (each cumsum sums in field arithmetic); (d) regression test with Complex64 input + Complex64 cotangent, assert resulting gradient stays Complex64 and is not all-zero; (e) workspace tests + clippy clean.
close_reason: Cumsum VJP now preserves dtype: complex inputs use (re,im) f64-pair accumulation re-encoded at input's complex dtype, real inputs keep f64 reverse-cumsum but re-encode at input's natural dtype (F32/BF16/F16/F64/I32/I64/U32/U64). Bool falls back to F64 per AD convention. Output TensorValue dtype = input dtype (except Bool→F64). 2 regression tests: Complex64 reverse-cumsum produces correct values AND keeps dtype + F32 input keeps F32. fj-ad 277 tests pass; clippy clean. Committed 9d1e46b.

title: Make batched fixture timeouts return without joining timed-out workers
description:
Review finding [HIGH]. Commit 0985d27 changed run_transform_fixture_bundle_batched in crates/fj-conformance/src/lib.rs to record timed-out worker handles, but the function then joins all timed_out_handles before returning (current lines around 1145-1186). That means case_timeout does not bound wall-clock runtime: a timed-out case with a long or hung worker still blocks the whole batched run at the final join.

Reproduction to add: construct a TransformFixtureBundle with one case having simulated_delay_ms = 5000 and BatchRunnerConfig { case_timeout: Duration::from_millis(1) }. Measure elapsed time for run_transform_fixture_bundle_batched. Expected behavior for a timeout runner is to return a Timeout report promptly after roughly the configured timeout; current behavior waits for the delayed worker during the final join and returns only after the worker finishes. A genuinely hung worker would hang the harness indefinitely despite emitting a timeout report.

Suggested fix: preserve bounded fan-out without joining timed-out workers on the critical return path. Options include a scoped worker pool with cooperative cancellation, a persistent bounded runner, or clearly documenting and renaming the timeout as classification-only. Add an elapsed-time regression test so future code cannot reintroduce timeout-as-blocking-join semantics. Validate with rch cargo test -p fj-conformance test_batch_runner.
close_reason: Detached timed-out batch workers from the return path and added elapsed-time regression coverage

title: Batch runner timeout leaks between test cases
description:
## Problem
The `test_batch_runner_marks_timeout` test is failing because timeout state leaks between test cases.

Test setup:
- Case 1: `batch_timeout` with `simulated_delay_ms: 200`, timeout of 50ms → Expected: Timeout
- Case 2: `batch_fast_after_timeout` with `simulated_delay_ms: 0`, same 50ms timeout → Expected: Pass

Actual behavior: Case 2 is being classified as Timeout instead of Pass.

## Location
`crates/fj-conformance/src/lib.rs:2203` - `test_batch_runner_marks_timeout`

## Root cause
The batch runner likely isn't resetting timeout state between cases, or there's a timing issue with how the timeout check is implemented.

## Fix needed
Review `run_transform_fixture_bundle_batched` to ensure timeout tracking is per-case, not cumulative.

title: fj-ad: xlogy/xlog1py grad wrongly zeroed x-derivative at x=0
description: xlogy/xlog1py VJP and JVP used where(x==0, 0, g*log(y)) for the x-gradient. But JAX's custom_jvp gives d/dx = log(y) (resp log1p(y)) with NO x==0 mask — the x-derivative is the analytic slope everywhere. So grad wrt x at x=0,y=2 gave 0 instead of log(2). Fixed all 4 (xlogy VJP+JVP, xlog1py VJP+JVP) to drop the select, matching JAX exactly (incl. inf/NaN edges at y=0/-1).

One lever only: compile the exact single-equation vmap(Eigh) dispatch shape into a guarded dispatch-entry artifact that bypasses avoidable generic transform/interpreter overhead while still producing the same cache key, evidence ledger semantics, output ordering, eigenvalue order, eigenvector sign convention/golden SHA, and errors for nonmatching requests. title: Implement cumprod JVP parity
description: Replace the cumprod JVP placeholder that applies cumsum to tangents with an axis-aware forward recurrence: d_y_i = d_y_{i-1} * x_i + y_{i-1} * dx_i. Cover scalar, vector, and rank-2 axis cases, including zero-aware behavior.
close_reason: Completed cumprod JVP parity

title: [code-review] public compile options can spoof custom VJP rule keys
description: Review finding from commit 201b835: ComposedTransform::with_compile_option is public and accepts the reserved key custom_vjp_rule_key. Repro: create a custom_vjp wrapper, read its rule key from an in-crate test, then call compose(the same Jaxpr, vec![Transform::Grad]).with_compile_option("custom_vjp_rule_key", &wrapped.rule_key).call([3.0]). The plain composed grad uses the wrapper's private VJP rule instead of ordinary AD, so wrapper-scoped derivative callbacks can be injected through user compile options. Strip reserved custom-derivative keys from user compile options before dispatch and add a regression test.
close_reason: Completed: stripped reserved custom VJP rule compile option from public composed transforms and added regression coverage for spoofed wrapper-scoped VJP keys.

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-lax linalg: contiguous-f64 real path for QR/LU/eig/SVD + blocked/recursive Cholesky-QR at scale
description:
Two-part algorithmic swing on fj-lax/linalg.rs (independent of mcqr.30 dense TensorValue storage — these kernels operate on extracted scratch buffers).

PART A (bit-exact, generalizes e6cd5c5c): the real fast paths in eval_qr/eval_lu/eval_eig/eval_svd/jacobi_eigendecomposition still accumulate O(n^3) inner loops over Vec<(f64,f64)> scratch, striding .0 by 16 bytes. Factor each real case in a contiguous Vec<f64> (same sequential accumulation order => bit-identical, like Cholesky e6cd5c5c gave ~1.25x at n=128). One lever per kernel/commit. Target ~1.2-1.5x each, more at large n.

PART B (algorithmic, needs parity-tolerance design): blocked/recursive right-looking Cholesky + blocked Householder-WY QR for the memory-bound n>=512 regime (cache-oblivious tiling from /alien-graveyard). These REORDER FP accumulation so they are NOT bit-identical to the scalar path — requires relaxing the internal cholesky_real_fast_path_bit_identical invariant to the conformance-oracle JAX tolerance (verify oracle is tolerance-based, not bit-exact, first). Target 2-4x at n>=512. Also: SVD currently eigendecomposes A^H A (squares condition number) — Golub-Kahan bidiagonalization or one-sided Jacobi directly on A is both faster AND more accurate vs JAX; check svd_oracle tolerance.

Baseline harness exists: crates/fj-lax/benches/lax_baseline.rs has linalg/cholesky_128x128, linalg/eig_48x48, linalg/matmul_2d_256. Add qr/lu/svd bench cases at n=128 and n=512.

title: [perf] fj-lax SVD real-valued AtA/Jacobi fast path
close_reason: Shipped: cyclic-Jacobi real thin-SVD fast path. ROOT CAUSE of pass49 marginal result: classic max-pivot Jacobi does an O(n^2) sqrt-bound find-max search before EVERY rotation (O(n^4)), identical cost in real and complex paths — so real-storage alone was only 1.08x same-worker (pass49 rejection was ~correct, not just cross-worker noise). LEVER: replace max-pivot with row-cyclic sweep (O(n^3*sweeps)). Same-binary same-worker bench (svd_48x48): complex 3.39ms vs real 1.55ms = 2.18x median over 3 runs. Conformance: linalg_oracle 56, higher_rank 12, solve 9, fj-lax 1113 all green; reconstruction+spectrum parity test added.

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-lax eigh/complex-eig: row-cyclic Jacobi (kill O(n^4) max-pivot search)
description: Generalize the SVD win (ec305146, 2.18x). The real jacobi_eigendecomposition (used by eval_eigh real path) and complex_jacobi_eigendecomposition (complex eigh + the still-complex full_matrices SVD path) both still use classic MAX-PIVOT Jacobi: an O(n^2) sqrt-bound find-max scan before EVERY rotation -> O(n^4), search-dominated at n>=48. Lever: row-cyclic sweep like jacobi_eigendecomposition_cyclic in linalg.rs (O(n^3*sweeps), stable symmetric-Schur coeffs Golub&VanLoan 8.4.1). NOT bit-identical (reorders FP) but eigh/SVD conformance is reconstruction+spectrum based (linalg_oracle.rs, no golden U/V fixture) so parity-safe. Prove via spectrum-match + V diag(w) V^T = A reconstruction. Bench same-binary same-worker (add a complex_path sibling like svd_48x48_complex_path), 3x median, Score>=2.0. Targets: eigh real n=48, complex eigh, and porting full_matrices SVD to the cyclic real path.
close_reason: Shipped real-eigh cyclic Jacobi (the benched hotspot). eval_eigh real path (m!=3) now calls jacobi_eigendecomposition_cyclic instead of max-pivot jacobi_eigendecomposition. Same-worker ts2 bench (new linalg/eigh_48x48_f64): max-pivot 5.62ms -> cyclic 1.33ms median (3 runs, stable 1.37/1.33/1.33) = ~4.2x. O(n^4) per-rotation search -> O(n^3*sweeps). Parity: eigh conformance reconstruction+spectrum (eigh_4x4/5x5, metamorphic_eigh_reconstruction, linalg_oracle 56, higher_rank 12) all green. Remaining (complex eigh complex_jacobi + full_matrices SVD complex path) split to follow-up.

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-lax complex_jacobi: row-cyclic sweep (complex eigh + complex/full SVD)
description: Last max-pivot Jacobi holdout. complex_jacobi_eigendecomposition (linalg.rs ~1139) still scans all O(n^2) off-diagonals before every rotation (O(n^4)). Used by: complex eigh (eval_eigh is_complex path), complex SVD (eval_svd), and the full_matrices REAL SVD path. Convert to row-cyclic sweep like jacobi_eigendecomposition_cyclic, but keep the Hermitian phase rotation (magnitude apq_abs + phase e^{i*phi}; see existing complex_jacobi body). Parity: reconstruction + spectrum (complex eigh/SVD conformance is tolerance-based). Bench same-binary (add complex eigh/SVD cases), NOT cross-invocation criterion baselines (rch fresh target per job). Score>=2.0. Also lets full_matrices real SVD move to the cyclic real path once a real orthonormal-column extension is ported.
close_reason: REJECTED (regression, reverted). Converting complex_jacobi_eigendecomposition to row-cyclic regressed the complex SVD path on ts2: maxpivot 3.39ms -> cyclic 5.9ms (1.7x SLOWER), 2 runs. Root cause / KEY LEARNING: cyclic-vs-maxpivot tradeoff is rotation-cost dependent. REAL paths (eigh 4.2x, SVD 2.18x): rotation is cheap real mul, so the O(n^2) sqrt-bound max-pivot SEARCH dominates -> removing it wins. COMPLEX path: each rotation is ~6 complex_muls (4mul+2add each) so rotation dominates, and cyclic does ~3x MORE rotations (all pairs every sweep) than greedy max-pivot (one biggest per iter) -> cyclic loses. Conformance passed (parity fine) but it's slower, so not shipped. Better complex levers split to follow-up: (a) route full_matrices REAL SVD (A^HA is real symmetric) to the real cyclic path; (b) Householder tridiagonalization + real QR for genuine complex Hermitian.

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-lax: route full_matrices REAL SVD to real cyclic path (port orthonormal-column extension)
description: Thin real SVD already uses the fast real cyclic-Jacobi path (ec305146). full_matrices=true real SVD still falls through to the complex max-pivot kernel only because the U orthonormal-column extension (complex_extend_unitary_columns) was not ported to real. Port a real extend_orthogonal_columns variant (one exists at linalg.rs ~1160 but its Gram-Schmidt normalization differs), gate the real fast path on full_matrices=true too, and verify reconstruction U diag(S) V^T = A + orthonormal U. Bench same-binary real vs complex_path with full_matrices. NOTE from p1vbf.21: do NOT make complex_jacobi cyclic (regresses 1.7x); this is purely about keeping REAL data on the real path.
close_reason: Shipped. Generalized eval_svd_real_thin -> eval_svd_real(full_matrices): full_matrices REAL SVD now uses the contiguous-f64 cyclic-Jacobi path, extending U's k economy columns to m×m via real Gram-Schmidt (extend_orthogonal_columns, previously dead_code). Same-binary same-worker ts2 bench (new svd_48x48_full_f64 vs svd_48x48_full_complex_path): complex 3.34ms vs real 1.53ms = ~2.17x median (2 runs). Parity: test extended to full_matrices (reconstruction U diag(S) V^T=A, U cols + V^T rows orthonormal, spectrum match + shape match vs complex). Green: fj-lax 1113, linalg_oracle 56, higher_rank 12. complex_jacobi untouched (cyclic regresses it per p1vbf.21).

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-lax: cache-blocked right-looking Cholesky for large n

Same-worker ts2: scalar cholesky_1024 231ms -> blocked 110ms = 2.1x (trailing GEMM auto-threads at n>=~768). n=512 is 1.72x (trailing updates below matmul_2d's 67M-FMA threading threshold; still faster). title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-lax: blocked QR needs rank-k panel microkernel (generic matmul_2d regresses)
description: Attempted compact-WY blocked Householder QR (gated n>=256). REGRESSED at n=1024: full-blocked 420ms, hybrid (blocked R + unblocked Q) 318ms, vs unblocked 244ms (ts2). Causes: (1) Q-formation via block reflectors over all q_cols does ~4x work of structure-exploiting unblocked loop; (2) even blocked R-only regressed — per-panel trailing GEMMs (K=nb=128, ~117M FMAs) are in matmul_2d's awkward zone (too little work/core to amortize threading, same regime that regresses 256^3) plus extract/scatter overhead; (3) unblocked rank-1 QR application is already competitive. Reverted (no regression shipped). Proper fix: dedicated rank-k panel-update microkernel (register-blocked, no per-panel matmul_2d thread fan-out) or n>=2048 regime; proper orgqr with column-restricted Q block-apply. Code/test were correct (reconstruction+orthonormality passed) — only perf failed.
close_reason: Completed: column-tiled real QR Householder reflector microkernel shipped. Same-worker RCH ts2 linalg/qr_1024x1024_f64 improved 246.75 ms to 51.927 ms mean (4.75x; conservative 4.49x). Behavior proof: per-column FP row order unchanged, focused bit-identity test, QR golden sha256 6119fc5cf4759d8cdcd9c34d89a79de89d205203730814fc06aa52bf57ff262b, fj-lax QR tests 10/10, linalg_oracle QR 8/8, higher_rank QR 2/2, check/clippy clean.

title: [perf][no-gaps] fj-lax: blocked LU correct but 1.43x (GEMM-throughput capped, rejected)
description: Implemented cache-blocked right-looking LU with partial pivoting (panel factor + pivot, L11^-1 U12 forward solve, A22-=L21 U12 via matmul_2d), gated n>=256. CORRECT (P A=L U reconstruction green, fj-lax 1115, linalg_oracle 56, higher_rank 12, solve 9 all pass). But same-worker ts2 lu_1024: unblocked 149ms -> blocked 104ms = 1.43x (nb=128; nb=64 worse at 127ms). REJECTED (<2.0). Why lower than Cholesky's 2.1x: unblocked LU is already cache-decent (149ms vs Cholesky's slow 231ms sqrt/strided), so less headroom; ceiling is matmul_2d throughput (~7 GFLOP/s). Note: blocked LU pivots can differ from scalar by rounding (different elimination order) but P A=L U holds; large-n LU has no exact-pivot conformance golden, small-n stays unblocked. Reverted. Real unlock = faster GEMM microkernel (see GEMM microkernel bead).
notes: SHIPPED 1cb7b1fb: cache-blocked right-looking LU (panel factor + U12 forward-solve + A22-=L21·U12 via fast matmul_2d), gated min(m,n)>=256. lu_1024 153.96→58.60ms=2.63x on ts1 (the GEMM microkernel f4c2a87f unblocked it — old blocked was 1.43x against the ~7 GFLOP/s matmul ceiling). Small-n stays scalar (bit-identical invariant + goldens preserved). New test lu_blocked_path_reconstructs_and_matches_scalar: blocked==scalar to 1e-9, P·A=L·U to 1e-7 at n=300; 55 lu + 80 linalg tests green. Score 2.63 >= 2.0.
